_purpose: Educational content for the Knowledge Hub material truths page. Conventional-fibre LCA numbers come from IMPRO 2014 / Quantis 2018 (consistent with backend reference_data.json). Preferred-alternative numbers and narrative text come from Textile Exchange Preferred Fiber Reports, Lenzing Tencel LCAs, Aquafil Econyl LCAs, and the Higg MSI library.
sources: Textile Exchange — Preferred Fiber & Materials Market Report 2023
Lenzing AG — TENCEL Lyocell life-cycle assessment
Sustainable Apparel Coalition — Higg Materials Sustainability Index

alternatives.label: Organic cotton
alternatives.why_better: Grown without synthetic pesticides or fertilisers, mostly rainfed rather than irrigated. Soil health improves over rotation cycles, and farmers avoid the most acutely toxic chemicals.
alternatives.caveats: Each field grows about a quarter less cotton, so more land is needed for the same number of clothes. It's also pricier, and only about 1% of cotton grown worldwide is certified organic.

alternatives.label: Recycled cotton
alternatives.why_better: Skips the entire farming, ginning and spinning chain by mechanically shredding old textiles. Diverts cotton from landfill in the process.
alternatives.caveats: Shredding old fabric breaks the cotton into shorter pieces, so recycled cotton is usually mixed with new cotton to keep the garment durable.

alternatives.label: Recycled polyester (rPET)
alternatives.why_better: Made from melted-down PET bottles, skipping crude-oil extraction, polymerisation, and most of the energy-heavy upstream stages.
alternatives.caveats: Still releases tiny plastic fibres when you wash it. The bottles it's made from could also have been recycled into new bottles instead, which keeps that plastic in a cleaner loop for longer.

alternatives.label: Recycled nylon (Econyl)
alternatives.why_better: Regenerated from discarded fishing nets, carpet scraps, and industrial nylon waste through chemical depolymerisation. Same performance as virgin nylon, indefinitely recyclable.
alternatives.caveats: It's still a plastic, so it still sheds tiny fibres in the wash. The recycling process also uses a lot of energy — just less than making nylon from scratch.

alternatives.label: Hemp
alternatives.why_better: Hemp is one of the highest-yielding plant fibres and grows on poor soil with minimal pesticides. The whole plant is used (fibre, seed, oil), and it actively sequesters carbon while growing.
alternatives.caveats: Uses much more water than acrylic to make the fabric — most of it during the soaking step that breaks the plant down into usable fibre. The texture also feels rough unless it's heavily softened.

alternatives.label: TENCEL Lyocell
alternatives.why_better: Made from FSC-certified wood pulp using a closed-loop solvent process — over 99% of the solvent is recovered and reused, versus viscose's open-loop carbon-disulfide process.
alternatives.caveats: Tencel is a brand name with a specific clean-recycling process. Generic 'lyocell' on a label might be made differently — look for the Tencel logo to be sure.

alternatives.label: Recycled wool
alternatives.why_better: Eliminates the entire farm-to-yarn stage by reclaiming wool from end-of-life garments. Italy's Prato district has been doing this for over a century.
alternatives.caveats: Recycled fibres are shorter and a bit weaker, so the fabric is usually mixed with new wool to hold its shape. Supply is also limited.

alternatives.label: Peace silk (Ahimsa)
alternatives.why_better: Silkworms are allowed to emerge from the cocoon as moths before the silk is harvested, eliminating the conventional practice of boiling the cocoons alive.
alternatives.caveats: When the moth flies out it breaks the silk thread, so each cocoon yields shorter pieces. That makes peace silk pricier and a little less smooth than regular silk.

fibres.label: Linen
fibres.preferred_note: Linen (flax) is already one of the lowest-impact fibres — rainfed, grown on poor soil that wouldn't sustain food crops, with the whole plant used (fibre, seed, oil-cake feed). The 'alternative' here isn't a different fibre, it's choosing European-grown, dew-retted, GOTS-certified linen.
fibres.caveats: Linen creases easily. Some manufacturers use chemicals to break down the flax stalks faster, which pollutes more water — look for linen labelled 'European' or 'dew-retted' for a cleaner version.
